items.quote: 我想用chatgpt image2 帮助淘宝、京东、AWS、跨境电商的卖家优化产品展示图片。
items.summary: Founder wants to use ChatGPT/GPT Image 2 to optimize marketplace product display images for China and cross-border sellers.
items.actorRole: founder
items.segment: marketplace sellers
caveats: Founder hypothesis, not buyer evidence.

items.summary: US Google Trends run over 53 weekly samples: product photography avg 52.4/latest 25/momentum -16.2; AI product photography avg 44.1/latest 15/momentum -23.7; product photo editing avg 40.8/latest 9/momentum -11; Amazon product images avg 30.9/latest 11/momentum -10; ecommerce photo editing avg 5.9/latest 9/momentum +5.3.
items.actorRole: search market
items.segment: US ecommerce searchers
caveats: Google Trends is normalized relative interest, not absolute search volume or willingness to pay.
Recent momentum is negative for the broader product image terms.

items.summary: OpenAI documentation says the API can generate and edit images with GPT Image models, including gpt-image-2; the Image API supports generation and edits, and the Responses API supports conversational image workflows.
items.actorRole: platform
items.segment: AI image generation
caveats: Capability evidence, not demand evidence; pricing and limits must be rechecked before launch.

items.summary: OpenAI lists GPT Image 2 as a text-and-image input model with image output and image edit endpoints.
items.actorRole: platform
items.segment: AI image generation
caveats: Requires API access, rate-limit, cost, and policy review.

items.summary: Amazon seller guidance says each detail page needs at least one image, recommends six images, and requires main images to show only the product on a white background; products should fill at least 85% of the image.
items.actorRole: platform
items.segment: Amazon sellers
caveats: Forum guidance is official Amazon staff content but should be checked against Seller Central help before launch.

items.summary: Pixc positions ecommerce photo editing for Shopify, Amazon, Etsy, and other stores, with credit-package pricing such as 10 basic images for $20 and 50 basic images for $100.
items.actorRole: paid alternative
items.segment: ecommerce merchants
caveats: Competitor pricing is not proof this product can charge; compare quality and workflow depth.

items.summary: JD has an official rule-center page relevant to marketplace rules, but the page requires JavaScript and the exact image requirements were not extracted in this run.
items.actorRole: platform
items.segment: JD merchants
caveats: Needs manual extraction or seller-console verification before using as a product rule set.

items.quote: I spend 30-60 minutes per SKU making a compliant image set, and I still worry the marketplace will reject the main image.
items.summary: Mocked seller pain for pipeline exercise: image production is repetitive, platform-specific, and compliance-sensitive.
items.actorRole: cross-border seller
items.segment: small Amazon seller
caveats: Mock data for workflow run; replace with real interviews.

items.quote: I would pay for one SKU pack if it guarantees the main image and secondary images follow marketplace rules.
items.summary: Mocked paid-intent result for pipeline exercise: 3 of 10 qualified sellers would try a paid SKU image pack at a low entry price.
items.actorRole: cross-border seller
items.segment: small Amazon seller
caveats: Mock data; must be replaced by real paid audit or preorder evidence.

items.quote: The current setup breaks when one API changes and we manually reconcile the spreadsheet every Friday.

Hey HN,

I made Browser-Use, an open-source tool that lets (all Langchain supported) LLMs execute tasks directly in the browser just with function calling.

items.quote:
Hey HN,

As a freelancer, I found myself spending time every month manually opening PDF and Word invoices, copying the details into a spreadsheet, and tracking payments. It was tedious and error-prone.

I decided to build a simple desktop app to automate this. It's a GUI built with Python and Tkinter that points to a folder of invoices, parses the key
items.summary: Automation failure audit trail for small B2B onboarding workflows: Show HN: I got tired of spreadsheets, so I built a Python GUI to track invoices

items.quote:
Hi HN,

We’ve been working on credit-based billing systems, and one recurring issue we kept running into was how messy credit purchases become once real payments are involved.

Credits are easy when they’re manual or promotional. Things get complicated when customers need to: - buy credits through a proper checkout, - receive an invoice, - complete payment
items.summary: Automation failure audit trail for small B2B onboarding workflows: Show HN: Checkout Flow for credit-based billing
items.actorRole: small B2B operators using automation workflows
items.segment: small B2B operators using automation workflows
caveats: Discovery evidence only; validate direct buyer pain and paid intent before building.

items.quote:

I built a small invoice tool for an internal requirement at my company.

items.summary: Automation failure audit trail for small B2B onboarding workflows: Show HN: A simple invoice tool I built instead of full accounting software
